11 Fifth Avenue | "Ghosts of the Brevoort"


MLA BIBLIOGRAPHY & LYRICAL PHRASE EXPLANATIONS

[Verse 1]


[26] "Eleven Fifth Ave, where the old hotel stood" → Inspired by: "Brevoort Apartments 11 Fifth Avenue

1953-65 BOAK & RAAD" Hennessey. (2022) Fifth Avenue, p. 28.

[7] "Built in eighteen-fifty-four, tiled floors and neighborhood" → Drawn from: "Between 8th and 9th Street, on the eastern side of the Avenue, stood the Brevoort Hotel, with its tiled floors and its celebrated sidewalk cafe. Built in 1854, it survived for one hundred years when it gave way to an apartment house." Delaney. (1968) New York's Greenwich Village, p. 56.

[7] "Jenny Lind and princes walking through the door" → Drawn from: "Early guests of the Brevoort included Jenny Lind, the Swedish nightingale, Edward VII as the Prince of Wales, and Caruso." Delaney. (1968) New York's Greenwich Village, p. 56.

[32] "Sidewalk café famous, French cuisine galore" → Inspired by: "These two famous old French institutions which have never deviated from their policy of serving 'the best meals in New York.'" Chase. (1932) New York The Wonder City, p. 120.

[32] "Orteig paid Lindbergh, twenty-five grand for the flight" → Drawn from: "Mr. Raymond Orteig who won international fame by paying the prize he offered of $25,000 for the first successful flight across the Atlantic to Col. Chas. Lindbergh." Chase. (1932) New York The Wonder City, p. 120.

[33] "Whiskey flowing free on Prohibition's last night" → Drawn from: "cases upon cases of whiskey were given away on the night of June 30, 1919, the last day before Prohibition went into effect." Kayton. (1999) Radical Walking Tours of New York City, p. 62.

[33] "Padlocked in twenty-six but the liquor never stopped" → Drawn from: "the cafe was padlocked in 1926 for violating Prohibition, illegal liquor flowed during Prohibition, particularly in the Village." Kayton. (1999) Radical Walking Tours of New York City, p. 62.

[33] "Village crowd kept drinking till the whole world dropped" → Inspired by: "illegal liquor flowed during

Prohibition, particularly in the Village." Kayton. (1999) Radical Walking Tours of New York City, p. 62.

[Chorus]


[26] "Glazed-brick giant rising where the legends fell" → Drawn from: "Another glazed-brick giant, built before the area was rezoned to ensure that new buildings did not overwhelm their historical neighbors." Hennessey. (2022) Fifth Avenue, p. 28.

[10] "Fourteen stories over Fifth, got stories time can tell" → Drawn from: "the buff-colored brick-clad Brevoort apartment house rose fourteen stories over the Fifth Avenue blockfront." Stern. (1995) New York 1960, p. 223.

[10] "From mansions to the wrecking ball, eighteen-fifty-four to fifty-three" → Drawn from: "its famous dining room and sidewalk café, the first such café to open after the repeal of Prohibition in 1933, functioned until 1953, when the building's demolition began." Stern. (1995) New York 1960, p. 223.

[28] "Ghosts of the Brevoort still haunt this block for me" → Inspired by: "It was not so long ago that the Brevoort Hotel was one of the landmarks at the comer of 8th Street and 5th Avenue. In 1954 it disappeared." McDarrah. (1963) Greenwich Village, p. 36.

[Verse 2]


[49] "Mark Twain in his white suit strolling on the porch" → Drawn from: "He had long been a fixture in the area, strolling up and down the Avenue in his well-known white suits and white shoes. While he lived at Number 21, he was often seen sitting on his front porch, talking to his cronies." James. (1971) Fifth Avenue, p. 38.

[20] "Twenty-one Fifth Avenue, biography by torch" → Drawn from: "It was at this house that Mark Twain embarked on the most significant literary project of his later years, his autobiography dictations and interviews for a biography published by Albert Bigelow Paine." Reiss. (2020) Lost New York, p. 101.

[20] "Billiards room and dictations, half a million words" → Drawn from: "The house included a billiards room, converted from a bedroom, where Sam could play his favorite sport" and "Over three years, Paine and his secretary would take down more than a half-million words of dictation." Reiss. (2020) Lost New York, p. 101.

[20] "Paine took notes while the old man's voice was heard" → Drawn from: "Paine was a regular visitor to Fifth Avenue, taking strolls with the white suit-clad author, taking notes while Sam sat in bed." Reiss. (2020) Lost New York, p. 101.

[1] "Mabel Dodge at twenty-three shocking every neighbor" → Drawn from: "No. 23 became famous as the home of Mabel Dodge, a wealthy heiress who was one of the great patrons of the original Bohemians in the 1910's." Delaney. (1968) New York's Greenwich Village, p. 55.

[1] "Bohemian salons where the young minds found their labor" → Inspired by: "No. 23 became famous as the home of Mabel Dodge, a wealthy heiress who was one of the great patrons of the original Bohemians in the 1910's." Delaney. (1968) New York's Greenwich Village, p. 55.

[3] "Sara Teasdale's last home, delicate and rare" → Drawn from: "The last home of Sara Teasdale, a woman of rare and delicate fancy, who came from her native St. Louis to New York, there to win a place quite her own among the verse makers of her period, was at 1 Fifth Avenue." Wilson. (1947) New York in Literature, p. 71.

[2] "One by one the old houses vanished in the air" → Drawn from: "the lower Fifth Avenue area where the old houses were giving way, one by one, to new luxury apartments." Delaney. (1968) New York's Greenwich Village, p. 118.

[Bridge]


[17] "Buddy Holly moved in, apartment four-H" → Drawn from: "By 1958 Buddy Holly was ready for a change... By September the couple moved into a spacious one-bedroom apartment with a balcony in the Brevoort." Katz & Kott. (2018) Rock and Roll Explorer Guide to New York, p. 116.

[5] "Balcony overlooking where the children play" → Drawn from: "killed himself in his home in one of his tall buildings overlooking Washington Square, where the children play." Walker. (1933) The Night Club Era, p. 281.

[17] "Peggy Sue Got Married on the apartment tapes" → Drawn from: "He also made some home recordings that were later dressed up with backing tracks known as the 'Apartment Tapes.' They include the tunes 'Peggy Sue Got Married' and 'Crying, Waiting, Hoping.'" Katz & Kott. (2018) Rock and Roll Explorer Guide to New York, p. 116.

[17] "Winter Dance Party took him, February's cruel fate" → Drawn from: "In January 1959 he would embark on his Winter Dance Party tour and never return. He was killed in a plane crash... on February 3, 'the Day the Music Died.'" Katz & Kott. (2018) Rock and Roll Explorer Guide to New York, p. 116.

[Outro]


[10] "Buff-colored brick where the Brevoort name still gleams" → Drawn from: "the buff-colored brick-clad Brevoort apartment house rose fourteen stories over the Fifth Avenue blockfront." Stern. (1995) New York 1960, p. 223.

[26] "Curved drive set back from the street like fading dreams" → Drawn from: "a central block with a curved drive is set back from the street and flanked by twin wings." Hennessey. (2022) Fifth Avenue, p. 28.

[39] "DeSapio and Tammany, the last boss fell right here" → Drawn from: "Walk west on Washington Place towards Washington Square Park... Take a right on Fifth Avenue and go past Eighth Street to the Brevoort Apartments, 11 Fifth Avenue, where Carmine DeSapio once had an apartment. Carmine DeSapio was the end of the line for Tammany Hall, the last boss of New York's most venerated-and vilified-political institution." Roth. (1996) Infamous Manhattan, p. 119.

[44] "Eleven Fifth Avenue — a century disappears" → Inspired by: "The name is that of the famous old Hotel Brevoort that occupied this location from 1854 until its demolition to make way for the present apartment house." Norton & Patterson. (1984) Living It Up, p. 72.
